coredata.dc:title: Ground state magnetic properties in AA-stacking bilayer graphene quantum dots using Lieb’s theorem
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials
coredata.dc:creator: Sharifian, M.
Hoseini, S.S.
Faizabadi, E.
coredata.dc:description: We have studied magnetic properties of ground state in perfect AA-stacking bilayer graphene quantum dots. Our main model is the single-orbital tight-binding Hamiltonian that is supplemented with a mean field Hubbard term. In addition, density functional method has been exploited to gain more confidence in our findings. The calculations are performed for some random and triangular shape of AA-stacking bilayer quantum dots and always yield an antiferromagnetic ordering with a total spin S = 0 ground state. Despite computational results of Hubbard model is admirably compatible with Lieb’s theorem, conventional crystallographic sublattices are not appropriate to interpret our computational results in terms of the theorem. Therefore, we have suggested a new sublattice decomposition to settle our numerical output to the consequences of Lieb’s theorem. Especially we demonstrate some characteristic including polarization of local magnetization, degeneracies in the energy spectrum, the ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic coupling of vacancies in our system have decisive compatibility with new proposed sublattice decomposition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hubbard model
Bilayer graphene
Edge magnetism

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Magnetism and Magnetic Materials
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bilayer graphene quantum dots in AA-stacking. (a) and (b) show different crystallographic sublattices in different color for a randomly created bilayer and a zigzag edges triangular bilayer quantum dot, respectively. (c) and (d) show different new sublattices for the same systems in (a) and (b).

Twins structures of single layer graphene nanoflake. (a) The “connection” interchanges sublattices in each twin. (b) Average local spin densities for a randomly created twins and (c) triangular twins graphene quantum dot (TTGQD) with zigzag edges. The radius of circles in (b) and (c) are roughly proportional to the local spin densities and different color correspond to different spin polarization.

Local spin densities. (a) For a triangular bilayer graphene quantum dot (TBGQD) with zigzag edges consist of 156 atoms using Hubbard model. (b) For a typical randomly created bilayer quantum dot using Hubbard model. In (a) and (b) blue (red) circles correspond to up (down) spin and the radius of objects are roughly proportional to the magnitude of local spin densities. (c) and (d) show DFT results for upper and lower layer respectively. In (c) and (d) blue (red) circles correspond to up (down) spin and bold (pale) colors roughly show the high (low) magnitude of local spin densities. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Side view of an optimized AA-stacking TBGQD with
N
edge
= 7
consisting of 156 atoms. AA-stacking remains unchanged. But the stacking lengths have a slight increase at the middle.
Local spin polarizations in AA-stacking multi-layer structures. (a) and (b) show four and three layer structures with total spin S = 0 and S = 3 respectively.
Layer magnetization for TGQD, TTGQD and BTGQD in terms of number of hexagon in each edge.

Mean field Hubbard model energy spectrums for (a) TGQD (b) TTGQD (c) TBGQD. (d) DFT results for single particle energies of TBGQD. Blue (red) symbols correspond to up (down) spin polarization. Meanwhile filled (empty) symbols correspond to occupied (unoccupied) single particle states. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Local magnetization across both layers of TBGQD, in presence of double vacancies. (a) and (b) show upper and lower layer respectively. The arrows indicate positions of vacant sites in each layer adopted from opposite sublattices (
A
'
B
'
). (c) and (d) are same layer in (a) and (b) instead vacant site in (c) shifted one site vertically. The radius of symbols roughly show the magnitude of local magnetization and the colors simultaneously correspond to different spin polarization and sublattices. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Ground state magnetic properties in AA-stacking bilayer graphene quantum dots using Lieb’s theorem

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Iran University of Science and Technology, 1684613114 Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Iran University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have studied magnetic properties of ground state in perfect AA-stacking bilayer graphene quantum dots. Our main model is the single-orbital tight-binding Hamiltonian that is supplemented with a mean field Hubbard term. In addition, density functional method has been exploited to gain more confidence in our findings. The calculations are performed for some random and triangular shape of AA-stacking bilayer quantum dots and always yield an antiferromagnetic ordering with a total spin S = 0 ground state. Despite computational results of Hubbard model is admirably compatible with Lieb’s theorem, conventional crystallographic sublattices are not appropriate to interpret our computational results in terms of the theorem. Therefore, we have suggested a new sublattice decomposition to settle our numerical output to the consequences of Lieb’s theorem. Especially we demonstrate some characteristic including polarization of local magnetization, degeneracies in the energy spectrum, the ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic coupling of vacancies in our system have decisive compatibility with new proposed sublattice decomposition.

ce:section.ce:para:
Low dimensional carbon-based nanostructures draw attention to theirs vast predicted properties and applications in physics and nano-scale technology [1–3]. The bedrock of some of these structures is graphene [4,5] that is a two-dimensional structure with two interpenetrating triangular crystallographic sublattices, namely A and B forming one honeycomb lattice. Also graphene helps to find a better conceptual understanding of other SP2 hybridized carbon based materials [6]. Although pristine graphene is nonmagnetic, its nano-islands have magnetic properties that well described in terms of single-orbital mean field Hubbard model including only Pz atomic orbitals forming π-bond states [7]. Literally Fernandez-Rossier and Palacios have shown interesting agreement between Hubbard model and DFT results [8]. One important graphitic carbon allotrope is bilayer graphene, composed of two graphene layers with two different basic layer stacking, namely AA and AB [9–11]. The AA one (the case of this paper) is straightforward, where each sublattice atom (A or B) in upper layer is exactly on top of the same sublattice atom of lower layer [12,13]. In comparison to graphene, infinite bilayer graphene has some distinctive features. Specifically a band gap in its electronic energy spectrum, besides the possibility of applying an extra perpendicular electric field to control its electronic and magnetic properties [10,14,15]. The magnetic properties of graphene allotropes is crucial to spintronic devices and in this regard bilayer graphene recently has captured more attentions [16–18]. Specially, antiferromagnetic spintronic itself has interesting advantages in information technology devices [19,20]. An insightful method for explaining magnetism in graphene quantum dots, is Hubbard model. In this context the emergence of magnetism in graphene quantum dots explained through a combination of Benzinoid graph theory and Lieb’s theorem [21]. Actually, two theorems about the ground state of this model is proved by E. H. Lieb [22]. Lieb’s theorems explain ground state magnetism through imbalance between two involved sublattices. The second theorem in Lieb’s paper states that for a constant repulsive onsite energy in bipartite lattice with even number of sites at half filling, the ground state is not degenerate and has spin
s =
N A
-
N B
/ 2
where NA and NB shows the number of sites in each party. The theorem has been used frequently to confirm the ground state total magnetization in single layer graphene quantum dots [8,23,24]. Also the theorem holds for AB-stacking bilayer graphene quantum dots in which two layer couples in a ferromagnetic ordering [25]. However we couldn’t find any paper focusing on Lieb’s approach to AA-stacking bilayer graphene quantum dots.

In this paper, by using Lieb’s theorem, we concentrate on evaluation and interpretation of ground state magnetic properties in AA-stacking bilayer graphene quantum dots. In furtherance of this aim, we discuss and show by theoretical manipulating of AA-stacking bilayer graphene quantum dot, it is possible to predict and explain its magnetic properties in the Lieb’s framework. As a result we have made a bridge between the vast deep captured physics of monolayer graphene and AA-stacking bilayer graphene. Therefore by generating many random singles, two, and multilayer graphene structures, we have tested the generality of our results. Besides, we candidate a bilayer triangular structure and a single layer associate counterpart for more comprehensive discussion.

In the present work, we form a general bilayer system by duplicating a randomly created monolayer graphene nanoflake (see Fig. 1 (a)). Likewise a zigzag triangular flake candidates for more comprehensive study (Fig. 1(b)).

In order to find the magnetic properties of mentioned systems, a single orbital Hubbard model Hamiltonian in the mean field approximation is exploited that given by
(1)
H = -
∑
i , j
, σ
t
i j
c
i , σ
†
c
j , σ
+
c
j , σ
†
c
i , σ
+ U
∑ i
n
i , ↑
n
i , ↓
+
n
i , ↓
n
i , ↑
-
n
i , ↑
n
i , ↓
the first term is the tight binding Hamiltonian in the well-known second quantization form. Indices i and j refers to summation over nearest-neighbor sites for intraplanar and interplanar with spin
σ = ↑ , ↓
.
c
i , σ
†
and
c
i , σ
are creation and annihilation operators for
P z
electron at site i with spin
σ
. The second term describes interactions between local density operators with a specific direction of spin in each site and the average local density of the opposite spin direction in the same site. U determines the strength of coulomb interaction and
n
i , σ
=
c
i , σ
†
c
i , σ
is number operator, accordingly local magnetic moment operator (z component of spin) defined as
(2)
s
i
z
=
n
i , ↑
-
n
i , ↓
2

So, for a fixed number of spin-up
N ↑
and spin-down
N ↓
electrons, a self consistent solution to Eq. (1) can be provided through starting from some random initial condition and reaching convergence to spin resolved average densities
〈
n
i , σ
〉
for all atoms and also, single particle energy spectrum
ε
n σ
. The procedure repeats for different combination of
N ↑
and
N ↓
until finding minimum energy with average densities. Total number of electrons is fixed and shows the neutrality of nanostructure. Here
N ↑
+
N ↓
=
N
is the total number of carbon atoms (half filling). Using mean field approximation reduces the big size of Hilbert space in Hubbard model to the size of system such that the Hamiltonian (Eq. (1)) is composed of two
N
×
N
matrices and
2 N
mean field parameters
〈
n
i σ
〉
. In tight-binding part of the Hamiltonian (Eq. (1)), hopping integrals have chosen
t
ij
=
t
intraplanar
= 3
( e V )
and
t
ij
=
t
interplanar
= 0.4
( e V )
in accordance with ref [26] using DFT calculations. Nevertheless, the onsite energy U is more arguable. A comparison study in ref [27] shows for moderate coulomb interaction (U/t < 2), ground state energies obtained from mean field approximation are in good quantitative agreement with exact diagonalization and Monte Carlo simulations. So, we have set U = 5. Also this choice enhances the local magnetizations further.

Moreover, to evaluate the reliability of our results, the magnetic calculations were carried out in the framework of density functional theory (DFT) [28] within Gaussian 03 program package [29]. Here the selected structure was fully optimized at the B3LYP [30,31] level using 6-31 g(d,p) basis set.

As a starting point we solve Hamiltonian (Eq. (1)) for a randomly created bilayer case and find the local spin densities that are shown in Fig. 3(b) for the ground state with total spin
S = 0
. This procedure was repeated for many randomly created nanoflakes and an antiferromagnetic order (total spin
S = 0
) was reached in all cases. For explaining how this situation comes about, we consider a randomly created twins flake which are connected with only one bond through a zigzag edge (Fig. 2
(a)).

Every randomly created twin nanoflake could have more atoms in one sublattice (A or B) but after creating the connection, A and B sublattices interchange in opposite twin flake and a balance situation will always be occurred in the whole flake. Consequently this situation always leads to
S = 0
net magnetization for ground state in the frame of Lieb’s theorem. Local magnetization results bear a striking resemblance to the bilayer case, such that, each layer of bilayer case and one of twins in the single layer are matched together (e.g. compare Figs. 2(b) and 3(b) or 2(c) and 3(a)). Trying to explain bilayer case, we consider a swap between sublattices in one layer relative to the other one shown in Fig. 1(c), then for AA-stacking bilayer, each atom from a sublattice, always has nearest- neighbors from the other sublattice atoms (intraplanar or interplanar) which is in accordance with Lieb’s theorem.

Actually, as the theorem states, the word “lattice” is misleading and necessarily dose not refer to a specific geometry. Instead it indicate two mutually interconnected assemblage of sites. Henceforth we label this new sublattice as
A
'
B
'
(Fig. 1(c) and (d)). So the total magnetization in AA-stacking bilayer in Lieb’s framework is
(3)
S =
N
A
'
-
N
B
'
2
where NA′ and NB′ shows the number of sites in each new party. We use prime to make no mistake with the famous AB Bernal stacking. In bilayer case, the word “sublattice” is referred to hereafter as
A
'
B
'
unless otherwise stated.

For a detailed study, we focus on triangular graphene quantum dots (TGQD) with zigzag edges. For a fixed number of atoms, this structure provides highest possible imbalance between two crystallographic sublattices and therefore leads to maximum magnetization for ground state. The number of atoms in each edge (
N
edge
) represents the size of triangle, furthermore it marks the number of single particle zero energies,
N Z
, such that
N Z
=
N
edge
-
1
. Then we continue to construct triangular twins graphene quantum dot (TTGQD) (Fig. 2(c)) and triangular bilayer graphene quantum dot (TBGQD) (Fig. 1(b)) both with zigzag edges. Fig. 1(b) shows conventional crystallographic sublattices of TBGQD in AA-stacking for
N
edge
=
7
consisting of 156 atoms. However in Fig. 1(d), a swap between sublattices in top (or bottom) layer redefines two appropriate sublattices of TBGQD consistent with Hubbard model in the frame of Lieb’s theorem
A
'
B
'
. As expected, we find local magnetization densities polarized to a certain direction (up or down) for TTGQD in each sublattice (A or B) (see Fig. 2(c)). Likewise, Fig. 3(a) shows similar pattern of local magnetization in TBGQD consistent with new sublattice decomposition
A
'
B
'
shown in Fig. 1(d). Additionally, many randomly created flakes were tested and the similar scenario was observed for all of them.

It is also worth to mention that we have performed DFT calculations on TGQD and TBGQD with zigzag edges consisting of 78 and 156 atoms respectively. After saturating and optimizing them, the C–C bond lengths in each layer remain almost unchanged (1.42
±
0.02
Å
). The relaxed TBGQD was obtained in AA stacking form too, but it is not completely flat and the upper and lower layers bend in the opposite direction and show a convex structure (see Fig. 4 ). The interlayer distance from edge to edge (d1) is about 3.4 Å, while in the central region (d2) is about 3.9 Å. Our calculations show that the spin septet
S = 3
and singlet
S = 0
states are the most stable states for TGQD and TBGQD respectively.

At the next step the magnetic calculations were carried out on the stable structures and the results confirm that the direction of local magnetization in DFT calculation and Hubbard model are in complete agreement. As it can be seen in Fig. 3(c) and (d), the maximum values of local magnetization belong to the edge atoms with about 0.286μB . And then by a gradual decrease, the minimum values are obtained around the center of medians of triangular with 0.101μB .

Here it is worthwhile continuing with more than two layer. To form a multi-layer structure (say M-layer), first, we take a randomly created single layer. Then configuring any desired stack of it (M copy) perfectly above each other one by one. Inevitably, two status may occur for total magnetization in an M-layers AA-stacking structure. (i) M is even or (ii) M is odd. (i) For even number of layers, the number of atoms in each sublattice (
A
'
B
'
) become equal. So the total magnetization turn into
S = 0
(Eq. (3)). Besides, every two adjacent layers experience alternate spin polarizations. Fig. 5 (a) shows local spin polarizations in a 4-layer AA-stacked of TGQD. (ii) For odd number of layers, the total magnetization of whole structure is equal to the magnetization of a single layer. Actually imbalance between sublattices in each layer compensate for the next layer up until last one. As a consequence, total imbalance for the whole structure is equal to the imbalance of a single layer Fig. 5(b).

In order to compare systems under consideration, we define layer magnetization
m
layer
=
∑
〈
s
i
z
〉
. Where summation includes all atoms in one layer of TBGQD or one twin of TTGQD or all atoms in TGQD. For TGQD the results of layer magnetization fulfill the anticipations of Lieb’s theorem
S
=
N A
-
N B
/ 2
where
N A
-
N B
=
N Z
. But by comparing TTGQD and TBGQD with TGQD, a little increase and a significant decrease were observed respectively (see Fig. 6 ). In TBGQDs, stacking of edges to the opposite layer leads to lose its edge-character and is responsible for a noticeable decrease of
m
layer
in each layer. Actually for any tested randomly created bilayer flake, we have seen that layer magnetization is less than single layer counterpart which is dictated by lieb’s theorem.

Meanwhile we proceed to investigate single-particle energy level spectrums. In the absence of coulomb interaction in TGQDs, there is a shell of degenerate zero energy state for both spin directions. Actually wave function of these states are mainly localized on the edge atoms leading to nontrivial so called edge magnetism [32]. After turning the interaction on, a HOMO-LUMO gap takes place between two spin-polarized shells (Fig. 7 (a)). However in TTGQD and TBGQD every energy state in spin–resolved spectrum is degenerate (Fig. 7(b) and (c)).

In other words for every energy, there is one spin-up and one spin-down state such that one filled shell of spin polarized edge state in TGQD with
N Z
= 6
changes to two half-filled shells with
N Z
= 12
state which are doubly degenerate for opposite spin directions. To clear up these degeneracies, recall our redefinition of sublattices for TBGQD in this paper
A
'
B
'
shown in Fig. 1(d). As a result of this definition, we see a perfect spatial symmetry between sublattices (this is not true if we choose classical crystallographic sublattices for AA-stacking bilayer graphene). Therefore, such a symmetry in spin-resolved energies was obtained. The similarity between the spectrums of TTGQD and TBGQD (Fig. 7(b) and (c)) is notable and both of them maintain the changing trend in single layer counterpart. Also spin-resolved energies from DFT calculation show high similarity to TTGQD and TBGQD spectrums (Fig. 7(d)). Once more our randomly selected structures have checked and their spectrums have shown similar degeneracies and trends. Furthermore, we have checked the validity of mentioned sublattices
A
'
B
'
when structures bear some vacancies. For this purpose, we examine systems with two vacant sites. In single layer structures two situations may occur if the vacancies belong to the same sublattice or not [33]. So, in TTGQD we can observe the fully expected results in which vacancies from the same sublattice lead to ferromagnetic coupling of spins. However, for vacancies in different sublattices, the coupling of spins are antiferromagnetic. Moreover for the ground state of TBGQD, the results are promising. In the first choice, we choose vacancies from nearest neighbor in opposite layer (two AA-stacked atoms from different layer) which belong to different sublattices in
A
'
B
'
. Local spin polarizations for upper (lower) layer of TBGQD have shown separately in Fig. 8 (a) and (b). Expectantly for the ground state, total spin is S = 0 with antiferromagnetic coupling between vacancies (Eq. (3)). However, in the second choice, the vacancy in upper layer is shifted one site vertically (Fig(c)). So both vacancies belong to the same sublattices in
A
'
B
'
decomposition. In this situation, the total spin is S = 1 and coupling is ferromagnetic (see Fig. 8(c) and (d)).

Our numeric results show for more than two vacancies, the ground state magnetization is predictable in accordance with Lieb’s theorem (Eq. (3)) when we choose
A
'
B
'
definition for sublattices. Specifically, for 3, 4 and 5 random vacancies in TBGQD, the local spin densities and total spin have been shown in Figs. S1–S3 in supplementary information.

Using both single-orbital Hubbard model and density functional theory, we have explained ground state magnetization in AA-stacking bilayer graphene quantum dots. For this purpose, by introducing a single layer counterpart to each bilayer flake, we have justified how an appropriate sublattice definition makes our numerical results in agreement with Lieb’s theorem. We have noticed an antiferromagnetic ordering for a perfect AA-stacking bilayer with
S = 0
net magnetization is predictable. In addition certain polarization of local magnetic moment in each atom is consistent with our choice of sublattices. Also total spin and local magnetizations in perfect multilayer AA-stacking is explainable. The spin-resolved energy spectrums show interesting degeneracies which is clearly related to spatial geometry of mentioned sublattices. All calculations based on single-orbital Hubbard model confirmed by DFT calculations. Finally we can anticipate ground state net magnetization of a perfectly AA-stacked bilayer in presence of vacancies if we adopt new proposed sublattices.
This work was supported by Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST) (Grant No. 160/482).
Edris Faizabadi ID http://orcid.org/0000-0002-9622-1232.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmmm.2019.01.057.
